In standard LTL (without counting or timestamps), the closest we can do is to enforce that if any of the props 1–4 has not occurred yet, then 'prop_0' cannot occur. Informally: "Never see prop_0 before seeing prop_1, prop_2, prop_3, AND prop_4". A classical encoding in LTL: if at a moment any of those is false and it is the first occurrence of prop_0, then the property does not hold. Alternatively, in a stronger sense: 'prop_0' can only occur if at some time in the past we've had all of 'prop_1', 'prop_2', 'prop_3', 'prop_4' become true. This is commonly encoded as '(!prop_1 | !prop_2 | !prop_3 | !prop_4) U (prop_1 & prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4)', globally before 'prop_0'. In LTL, we can say: it is always the case that 'prop_0' implies that in the past (possibly now), 'prop_1' and 'prop_2,' etc. have all happened. Since plain LTL has no past, the causal form is: It is always the case that until all four props occur, 'prop_0' cannot occur. That is, '!(prop_0)' until (prop_1 & prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4). To ensure this property holds always, wrap it using 'G'.

So, the best encoding is G((¬prop_1 | ¬prop_2 | ¬prop_3 | ¬prop_4) -> ¬prop_0).

explanation: The sentence states that 'prop_0' can only occur after each of 'prop_1', 'prop_2', 'prop_3', and 'prop_4' have occurred. Semantically, at any point in time, if 'prop_0' occurs, it should be the case that each of the others has already occurred previously or at the same moment. This is formalized by requiring that, at every moment, if 'prop_0' holds, then 'prop_1', 'prop_2', 'prop_3', and 'prop_4' must have held at some time in the past or now (i.e., 'historically'). In LTL, this is typically captured by using the 'globally' operator with 'if prop_0, then previously F prop_n,' for each prop. But since LTL lacks a 'past' operator, the best approximation is: globally, if 'prop_0' occurs, then before or at that point, each of 'prop_1', 'prop_2', 'prop_3', and 'prop_4' should have occurred at least once. Without past operators, the idiomatic LTL way is: 'for each prop_n, it is not possible for prop_0 to occur before prop_n does.' This is encoded as: 'not(prop_0) U prop_1', and similarly for the others. The conjunction of these expresses that 'prop_0' cannot happen until all four have happened, in any order, but at least once each.
